# hermes-plugin-sigil: agent install guide

You are an LLM agent (likely Hermes itself) helping a user install and configure
this plugin. Follow the steps below in order. Ask the user for any value you
do not have. Do **not** guess endpoints, regions, or tokens.

Repo: https://github.com/alexander-akhmetov/sigil-hermes
Plugin name (in `~/.hermes/config.yaml`): `sigil`
Hermes upstream: https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent

## What this plugin does

Records every Hermes LLM call and tool invocation to **Grafana Cloud AI
Observability** (called "Sigil" in package names and env vars). It has two
independent channels. You can configure either, both, or neither:

| Channel | What flows | Where it shows up |
|---|---|---|
| **Generations** | Per-LLM-call records with prompts/responses/tool calls | AI Observability → Conversations |
| **OTel traces + metrics** | `gen_ai.client.*` spans and metrics | Grafana Cloud Traces / Metrics data sources |

The two channels use two different endpoints but **share a single
Grafana Cloud access policy token**. As long as the token carries the
scopes for both (`sigil:write` plus the OTLP write scopes), the same
secret goes into `SIGIL_AUTH_TOKEN` and into the OTel `Authorization`
header.

### What conversation data leaves the machine (read this to the user)

By default this plugin sends **full conversation content** to Grafana Cloud:

- Every user prompt and every assistant response (text content).
- Every tool call's **arguments** and the tool's **result** (truncated per
  string at `SIGIL_HERMES_MAX_CHARS`, default 12000 chars).
- Conversation history snapshots that get attached to each generation as
  `input` so the UI can show the full turn.
- Token usage, model name, finish reason, and timing.

Tool args and results in particular can include filesystem paths, command
output, file contents, API responses, and anything else Hermes tools
produce. Treat the destination Grafana Cloud stack as a place this content
will live.

This is a deliberate choice. The SDK's own default is `no_tool_content`,
but the plugin sets `full` because tool args/results are usually the most
useful thing to see in the AI Observability UI.

The privacy lever is **`SIGIL_CONTENT_CAPTURE_MODE=metadata_only`**. Set
it before starting Hermes and the plugin still records every call, but
only counts, model name, usage, finish reason, and timing leave the
machine. No prompts, no responses, no tool args, no tool results. Both
channels honor it.

```bash
# Recommended for privacy-sensitive setups: only counts, model name,
# usage, timing. No prompts, responses, or tool I/O:
export SIGIL_CONTENT_CAPTURE_MODE=metadata_only

# Middle ground: keep prompts/responses, drop tool args + results:
export SIGIL_CONTENT_CAPTURE_MODE=no_tool_content

# Sample only a fraction of calls (defense-in-depth, not redaction):
export SIGIL_HERMES_SAMPLE_RATE=0.1   # 10%
```

The plugin does **no PII redaction**. It bounds payload structure
(depth, entries, per-string length) but does not pattern-match for
secrets, emails, or keys. If the user has compliance constraints, set
`SIGIL_CONTENT_CAPTURE_MODE=metadata_only`, or route through a Grafana
Alloy / OTel Collector that does redaction before forwarding.

## Step 1: Install into the same environment as `hermes`

```bash
which hermes                     # find the python that runs hermes
<that-python> -m pip install git+https://github.com/alexander-akhmetov/sigil-hermes
```

If `hermes` lives in a venv or pipx env, install into *that* env, not the
system Python. The plugin loads as an entry point. If it is in the wrong
env, Hermes will not see it.

## Step 2: Enable in `~/.hermes/config.yaml`

`hermes plugins enable sigil` does not currently see pip-installed plugins.
Edit the YAML directly:

```yaml
plugins:
  enabled:
    - sigil
```

If the file or `plugins:` block does not exist, create it. Preserve any
existing entries.

## Step 3: Collect credentials from Grafana Cloud

Tell the user: **"I need you to open three pages in Grafana Cloud and
copy five values: two endpoint URLs, two instance IDs, and one access
policy token. I will not guess them."** Then walk them through it.

### Prerequisite: enable the AI Observability plugin

If they have not already: in their Grafana Cloud stack go to
**Observability → AI Observability**, accept the terms, click **Save**.

### Page A: AI Observability → Configuration

URL pattern: `https://<stack>.grafana.net/plugins/grafana-sigil-app`

Ask the user to copy:

1. **API URL** → call this `SIGIL_ENDPOINT`. It looks like
   `https://sigil-prod-<region>.grafana.net`. Do **not** construct this from
   a region name. The user copies it verbatim from the page.
2. **Instance ID** → call this `SIGIL_AUTH_TENANT_ID`. Numeric Grafana Cloud
   stack ID.

### Page B: grafana.com → Security → Access Policies

URL: `https://grafana.com/orgs/<org>/access-policies`

Create **one** access policy token that covers both channels. The same
token will be reused in `SIGIL_AUTH_TOKEN` and in the OTel
`Authorization` header.

- **Name**: anything, e.g. `sigil-hermes`
- **Realm**: select the stack where AI Observability is enabled. The realm
  must match the stack's region.
- **Scopes** to add (the picker filters as you type):
  - `sigil:write` (generations channel)
  - `metrics:write`, `metrics:import` (OTel metrics)
  - `traces:write` (OTel traces)
  - `logs:write`
  - `alerts:write`, `rules:write`, `profiles:write` (parity with what
    the OTLP card's "Generate now" button creates, harmless to include)
- Click **Create**, then **Add token**, copy the token shown once.

3. The token from this page → call this `SIGIL_AUTH_TOKEN`. Starts with
   `glc_…`. Keep it. It's also the password half of the OTel basic-auth
   header below.

### Page C: Cloud portal → stack details → OpenTelemetry card

Only needed if the user wants traces and metrics (recommended, but optional).

URL: `https://grafana.com/orgs/<org>/stacks/<stack-id>`. Find the
**OpenTelemetry** card on that page.

Ask the user to copy:

4. **OTLP Endpoint URL** → call this `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT`. It looks
   like `https://otlp-gateway-prod-<region>.grafana.net/otlp`. Copy
   verbatim, do not construct.
5. The numeric **Instance ID** shown on the card. This is the basic-auth
   *username* for OTLP. It may match the AI Observability Instance ID from
   Page A, or it may not. Copy whichever number the card shows.

Do **not** click "Generate now" on this card. The Page B token already
covers OTLP scopes, and one token is easier to rotate than two. We only
need the Instance ID and endpoint URL from this page.

## Step 4: Set the env vars

These are the values Hermes needs in its process environment. **Ask the
user how they want to set them.** Some people put env vars in a shell
profile, others in a project `.envrc`, others pull from a secret
manager, others prefix `KEY=value hermes …` inline. Don't edit anything
on their behalf without confirmation.

```
# --- Generations channel (AI Observability → Conversations) ---
SIGIL_ENDPOINT          = <API URL from Page A>
SIGIL_PROTOCOL          = http
SIGIL_AUTH_MODE         = basic
SIGIL_AUTH_TENANT_ID    = <Instance ID from Page A>
SIGIL_AUTH_TOKEN        = <glc_... token from Page B>

# --- OTel channel (traces + metrics) ---
# Same token as SIGIL_AUTH_TOKEN. Basic-auth username is the OTLP
# Instance ID from Page C (which may or may not equal Page A's).
OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT = <OTLP endpoint from Page C>
OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS  = Authorization=Basic <base64 of "<otlp-instance-id>:<glc_... token>">
```

The OTel header needs the basic-auth value pre-encoded. To compute it:

```bash
printf '%s' '<otlp-instance-id>:<glc_token>' | base64 | tr -d '\n'
```

A trailing newline silently breaks the header. `tr -d '\n'` is not
optional.

Whatever mechanism the user picks, it must be in effect by the time
`hermes` starts so the plugin inherits the values.

### Optional knobs (do not set unless the user asks)

| Variable | Default | When to change |
|---|---|---|
| `SIGIL_AGENT_NAME` | `hermes` | Multiple Hermes deployments and you want to distinguish them in the UI |
| `OTEL_SERVICE_NAME` | `hermes` | Same reason |
| `SIGIL_CONTENT_CAPTURE_MODE` | `full` | Set `no_tool_content` or `metadata_only` to drop tool args/results from records |
| `SIGIL_DEBUG` | `false` | Set `true` for verbose SDK logs while debugging install |
| `SIGIL_HERMES_SAMPLE_RATE` | `1.0` | Lower if volume is high. Range `0.0`–`1.0` |
| `SIGIL_HERMES_MAX_CHARS` | `12000` | Truncation cap per redacted string |
| `SIGIL_HERMES_OTEL_AUTO` | `true` | Set `false` if the host app already installs `TracerProvider` / `MeterProvider` |

## Step 5: Verify

Run:

```bash
SIGIL_DEBUG=true hermes
```

Tail `~/.hermes/logs/agent.log`. On a clean install you should see:

```
hermes-plugin-sigil: installed TracerProvider with OTLP HTTP exporter
hermes-plugin-sigil: installed MeterProvider with OTLP HTTP exporter
hermes-plugin-sigil: Sigil client initialized (generations=configured, otel=configured)
```

If a channel is unconfigured the corresponding line says `unconfigured`
instead of `configured`. That is fine if the user opted out of that channel.

Have the user ask Hermes any question, then open **Grafana Cloud →
Observability → AI Observability → Conversations**. The generation should
appear within a few seconds. Check **Traces** (filter for
`service.name = hermes`) for the OTel side.

## Common failure modes

| Symptom | Likely cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Plugin lines never appear in `agent.log` | Installed into wrong Python env | Run `which hermes`, install into that env |
| Plugin loads but `generations=not_configured` | `SIGIL_AUTH_TOKEN` empty or `SIGIL_AUTH_MODE=none` | Re-export and reload shell |
| `401 Unauthorized` on generations | Access policy missing `sigil:write`, or wrong realm | Edit the Page B policy to include `sigil:write` and confirm the realm matches the stack |
| `401` on OTLP traces/metrics | Access policy missing OTel write scopes, or wrong basic-auth username | Edit the Page B policy to include `metrics:write` / `traces:write` / `logs:write`. Confirm the basic-auth username is the OTLP Instance ID from Page C, not the Sigil one from Page A |
| `404` on generations | `SIGIL_ENDPOINT` was hand-constructed with wrong region | Copy verbatim from AI Observability → Configuration |
| Traces/metrics silently absent | Host app installed its own provider, or `SIGIL_HERMES_OTEL_AUTO=false` | Either let the plugin install providers, or have the host app add OTLP exporters |

## Hard invariants for the agent

- **Never invent endpoints from a region string.** Both the Sigil endpoint
  and the OTLP endpoint must come from the user's Grafana Cloud UI, not
  from your own pattern-matching. Regions are not always literal (`prod-us`,
  `prod-eu`, `prod-ap-southeast-1`, etc.) and the URLs sometimes include
  extra segments.
- **One token covers both channels.** A single Page B access policy
  token, with `sigil:write` plus the OTLP write scopes, works for
  `SIGIL_AUTH_TOKEN` *and* as the password half of the OTel basic-auth
  header. Do not direct the user to "Generate now" on the OpenTelemetry
  card. They end up with two tokens to rotate for no benefit.
- **Never overwrite existing env vars without confirming.** If the user
  already has `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT` set for another collector,
  ask before changing it. The plugin will respect whatever provider the
  host installs if `SIGIL_HERMES_OTEL_AUTO=false`.
- **Stop and ask if any of the five values is missing.** Do not proceed
  with placeholder values. The plugin will fail open silently and the user
  will not know why no data shows up.
